ontology.foodon_gap_note: No exact FoodOn term for tandoori chicken salad; mapped to broader 'chicken dish' class (FOODON_03311069). A more specific term for tandoori-marinated grilled chicken salad does not exist in the current FoodOn release.
ontology.foodex2_description: Chicken meat, cooked — used as the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor for tandoori grilled chicken; salad composite not individually coded in FoodEx2.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for tandoori chicken salad. A0CKP (cooked chicken meat) is used as the closest parent anchor. The salad composite nature and oil-based dressing component are not captured by this code and are documented here.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT clinical food concept found for tandoori chicken salad as a composite dish.

main_ingredients: tandoori spices (turmeric, cumin, coriander, chilli, ginger, garlic)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of Tandoori Murgh Salad is approximately 250 g, comprising roughly 120–140 g of grilled tandoori chicken pieces on a bed of fresh lettuce and salad vegetables tossed with house dressing.

nutrient_source.title: Tandoori Murghsalad — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Tandoori Murghsalad (cr_id G00010893). All per-100g macro and micronutrient values are taken verbatim from the HPB record. Saturated fat is estimated at approximately 7.5 g/100g based on the total fat of 44.54 g/100g and the known fat profile of oil-based dressings combined with grilled chicken; this field is marked as adapted. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, and water are not reported in the HPB source and are set to -1.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — analogous estimate based on mixed salad with grilled chicken and oil-based dressing
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Tandoori Murgh Salad. GI estimated at approximately 32 based on the closest analogous food category: mixed green salad with grilled chicken and oil-based dressing. The dominant carbohydrate contributors are lettuce (GI ~15) and a small amount of marinade sugars; the high fat content (44.54 g/100g) from the dressing substantially suppresses glycaemic response. GL per serving calculated as: GI (32) × carbohydrate per serving (14.75 g for 250 g serving) / 100 = 4.7. Source: University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021 (international GI tables).

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 810 mg/100g — substantially exceeds the 600 mg/100g upper threshold for hypertension-safe foods
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit this dish; if consumed, request reduced salt in the tandoori marinade, replace oil-based dressing with lemon juice, and limit portion to no more than 100 g to keep sodium intake below 810 mg per occasion.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low carbohydrate (5.9 g/100g) and low GI (~32) are favourable, but very high total fat (44.54 g/100g) and estimated saturated fat (~7.5 g/100g) may worsen insulin resistance with regular consumption
type2_diabetes.guidance: The low carbohydrate content and low glycaemic index support blood glucose stability in the short term; however, individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should request dressing on the side or substitute with a lemon-herb dressing to reduce total fat and saturated fat intake, which can impair insulin sensitivity over time.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Very high total fat (44.54 g/100g), estimated saturated fat (~7.5 g/100g), sodium 810 mg/100g, and cholesterol 26 mg/100g collectively elevate cardiovascular risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should treat this dish as an occasional food only; request dressing on the side, use lemon juice as a substitute, and limit portion size to reduce saturated fat and sodium load, which together raise LDL cholesterol and blood pressure.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Tandoori Murgh Salad is an Indian-origin dish served in Singapore restaurants, consisting of boneless chicken pieces marinated in a spiced yogurt mixture — typically containing turmeric, cumin, coriander, chilli powder, ginger, and garlic — and cooked in a tandoor (clay oven) at high heat to produce a characteristic charred, smoky exterior with a moist, tender interior. The grilled chicken is served on a bed of fresh lettuce and salad vegetables, tossed with an oil-based house dressing. The dish has a bold, smoky, and mildly spiced flavour profile with a contrast of warm grilled chicken and cool crisp salad. Per 100 g, it provides 430 kcal, 1.32 g protein, 44.54 g fat, 5.9 g carbohydrate, 4.69 g sugar, 0.0 g dietary fibre, and 810 mg sodium. The very high fat content is primarily attributable to the oil-based dressing rather than the chicken itself. Sodium at 810 mg/100g is clinically significant and makes this dish unsuitable for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease without modification. The low carbohydrate content and low glycaemic index (~32) are favourable for blood glucose management, but the high saturated fat (~7.5 g/100g) may impair insulin sensitivity with regular consumption. Tandoori spices including turmeric and ginger provide anti-inflammatory phytochemicals. This dish is commonly consumed as a lunch or dinner item and is popular in Indian restaurants across Singapore.
common_user_queries: Is tandoori chicken salad good for diabetes?
Can I eat tandoori chicken salad if I have high blood pressure?
Low carb Indian chicken salad Singapore

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id G00010893, confidence 0.95), which is the primary approved source for Singapore dishes. Saturated fat is estimated from the known fat profile of oil-based dressings and grilled chicken and is clearly documented as adapted. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database framework, documented transparently.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (7.5 g/100g) is an adapted estimate based on the total fat of 44.54 g/100g and the typical fatty acid profile of oil-based salad dressings combined with grilled chicken; the HPB source does not report saturated fat separately. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown and moisture data for this composite dish are not available in the HPB Singapore Foodo database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2026-04-16. 3. dietary_fibre_g is reported as 0.0 by the HPB source; this is confirmed zero per the HPB record, though the presence of lettuce would typically contribute trace fibre — the HPB value is retained as reported. 4. protein_g at 1.32 g/100g is notably low for a chicken-based dish and likely reflects the high dressing-to-chicken ratio in the as-served composite; this value is taken verbatim from the HPB source. 5. GI of approximately 32 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this specific dish exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. 6. Halal status is assumed based on the dish composition (chicken, spices, vegetables) but is not formally certified in the HPB record.
